content:
5 min read
Just now

--

.
Work
Softwire is the kind of place where trust feels easy to build, which made initiating two difficult conversations this week feel even harder.
Out of the two, the toughest was about an eroding relationship with a colleague I respected and had relied on being candid with. Some things on the project hadn’t been working, friction had been surfacing in our ways of working, and I’d been carrying the weight of that erosion for a while. I knew I needed to share the impact it had on me, and my hope for reconciliation and a better way forward between us.
Not wanting to assert blame, and wanting the conversation to build on empathy rather than accusation, I turned to Claude AI Chat to be a neutral mediator with no stake in the relationship. I used it to sense-check my thinking and force myself to be more succinct: what did I actually want to convey, and what outcome was I hoping for?
Even so, I didn’t know how to start the conversation. I checked in with him on his thoughts about our working relationship before diving into where my mind was at and the points I’d prepared. He stayed quiet for the most part, but also shared his side and the context behind his behaviours and actions.
What emerged was that we’d each been operating from assumptions shaped by past experiences, about what good feedback looks like, what support means, what our respective roles required of each other. None of it communicated. All of it adding to the friction that led to this chat.
Once we’d each shared our reservations, fears, and needs, we were able to start properly shaping our working relationship, one that’s more aligned and grounded in understanding. I don’t want to share the specific details and risk confidentiality, but while the conversation was hard, it served its purpose: airing out a tension that had been blocking our relationship from moving forward. I felt a huge weight lift, and a sense that we’d regained a communication channel that feels more honest and vulnerable and which laid the foundation for repair, and perhaps even a stronger relationship going forward.
We left without concrete next steps, but with something more important: a shared acknowledgement that the relationship matters and is worth repairing.
There’s an adage that you don’t really know someone until you’ve faced a storm together. It’s the same with building trust, which is built on openness, honesty, and a bit of vulnerability. So while time will tell, I’m feeling hopeful.
Life
It’s the last week before my month-long holiday with my partner and all three boys. It’ll be the first time in Canada for my partner’s two older boys, Layton’s brothers, and they’re excited to explore and do all the tourist things.
I’ve been more stressed than excited planning the trip. This isn’t really a holiday for me so much as it is for the boys. A month sounds like ample time, but between a two-week road trip and the rest split between visiting scattered family and friends and life admin, there isn’t much wiggle room to fit everything in.
One of the most important, and most complicated, parts of this trip is seeing my grandparents, who are in their early nineties. My grandpa, who prided himself on his independence, had a fall last year. He has been bedridden since. My grandma, in her own nineties, has become his main carer, since he refuses additional help outside the family; my dad and my aunt take turns doing house visits. While Layton has met his great-grandparents before, it’ll be his brothers’ first time, and probably their last, since it’s unlikely we’ll do a trip like this with everyone in tow again. It’s a mixed feeling I can’t quite articulate. The boys won’t be able to communicate with my grandparents at all because of the language barrier, and with my grandparents keeping an even earlier bedtime than our four-year-old, the timing for visits gets tricky. Even without the language barrier, I struggle to sustain conversations longer than five minutes with them on our regular video calls. We’re not the most communicative family. A lengthy visit with my bedridden granddad would feel forced and awkward, and wouldn’t really work. I’m unsure what to do.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A photo from 3 years ago visiting my grandpa who lit up upon seeing his 1 year old great grandson.So no, aside from the road trip, this won’t feel much like a holiday for me. I want everyone to enjoy it, but travelling as a group of five, each wanting different things from the trip, isn’t going to be easy, and not everyone will enjoy it equally. I need to do myself a kindness and recognise that we won’t be able to fit everything in or appease everyone, and that’s okay.
Otherwise, I do look forward to the trip. I’m overdue for a reset from routines that have been a bit overwhelming lately. My to-do list has been growing dangerously because my appetite to do more outpaces my physical and mental capacity. Something to manage better. Another one to add to the list to look at when I’m back from leave. Ha!
Things
“Better output is not the same as learning.”
I love this quote from a post by Damien Rayuela on how AI can enable real learning.
One takeaway for me: how do we bake in the friction and effort that learning actually needs, when getting answers at speed is the default?
As answers get faster and more accurate with AI, the goalposts for learning shift from getting to answers, to being able to make the good decisions and judgment calls that produce good answers in the first place.
A finding that piqued my interest was that when feedback comes from a human rather than AI, we’re more likely to act on it. There’s something in knowing we’re being seen by another person that drives us to put in more effort and be better. Damien puts this beautifully: AI can teach the content. A human helps you become someone.
A lot of my writing comes back to how AI reshapes our identities, perspectives, and sense of self: what it enriches, and what it subtly takes away, some of it deliberate, most of it not. What does it mean for my job when AI can seemingly output better visual design than I can? What does it mean for my child, growing up in a world where AI is the default answer to his many curiosities instead of asking his parents? How do we show up for each other when care itself is increasingly delegated to machines? And when AI is everywhere, trying to solve all our problems, who’s accountable when things go wrong, and harm is caused?
I’m not the only one sitting with these questions and no clear answers. For now, I’m putting these thoughts down to focus on my impending leave.
A random fun aside. I had a fortune cookie this week that gave me a nice fortune which I hope to get realised :)

I hope it’s the good kind of thrilling!Weeknotes shall resume when I return!
